content:
The alert fired for six weeks. We treated it like disk space at 72%.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Copy-paste commands. No fluff.
SELECT datname, age(datfrozenxid), pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size(datname))
FROM pg_database
ORDER BY age(datfrozenxid) DESC;billing_prod — age(datfrozenxid): 1,982,000,000. Wraparound limit: 2,000,000,000. ~18 million transactions until shutdown.
Eleven days at current ingest rate. Maybe nine if Black Friday rehearsal traffic hit early.
Alert postgres_wraparound_warning fired six weeks ago. Ticket opened. Priority: P3. Assigned to backlog grooming.
You write it, I deploy it, I suffer when vacuum stops and Postgres refuses writes.
What wraparound actually means
Postgres transaction IDs are 32-bit. They wrap. autovacuum freezes old rows so XIDs don't collide.
When age(datfrozenxid) approaches 2 billion, Postgres enters single-user shutdown to prevent corruption. Not gradual degradation. Hard stop.
-- emergency visibility
SELECT datname,
age(datfrozenxid) AS xid_age,
2147483647 - age(datfrozenxid) AS xids_remaining
FROM pg_database
WHERE datname = 'billing_prod';
-- xid_age: 1982000000
-- xids_remaining: 165483647 (~9-11 days at 15M tx/day)P3 ticket. Six weeks. Nine days remaining when I got paged.
The six-week alert we ignored
Grafana panel: yellow at 1.5B, red at 1.9B. Yellow since February 8. Red since March 3.
PagerDuty: warning only, business hours, no page.
Why P3:
→ “Disk isn’t full” → “Queries still fast” → “Autovacuum will handle it” (it wasn’t — long transactions blocking freeze)
# what blocked vacuum
SELECT pid, state, xact_start, query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state != 'idle'
AND xact_start < now() - interval '2 hours';
-- 3 rows: analytics replica slot, BI tool, orphaned connection from staging cronLong transactions held back freeze on billing_events — 890 GB, 40% dead tuples.
Five-minute first response — what we should have run week one
-- 1. Age per database
SELECT datname, age(datfrozenxid) FROM pg_database ORDER BY 2 DESC;-- 2. Tables blocking freeze
SELECT c.relname, age(c.relfrozenxid) AS table_xid_age
FROM pg_class c
JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid = c.relnamespace
WHERE c.relkind = 'r' AND n.nspname NOT IN ('pg_catalog','information_schema')
ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 10;-- 3. Long transactions
SELECT pid, age(backend_xmin), query FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE backend_xmin IS NOT NULL ORDER BY 2 DESC;-- 4. Autovacuum progress
SELECT * FROM pg_stat_progress_vacuum;Run weekly. Not at 1.9 billion.
→ PostgreSQL War Room — wraparound is in the diagnosis tree. Emergency queries copy-paste ready. Would have been week-one reading, not week-six panic.
The remediation sprint — nine days
Day 1: kill orphaned staging cron connection. Terminate 2-hour BI queries during maintenance window.
Day 2–4: manual VACUUM FREEZE VERBOSE on billing_events in batches — 4-hour windows, 200 GB per night.
VACUUM (FREEZE, VERBOSE) billing_events;
-- progress: 200GB/night, xid_age dropping ~80M/day after blockers clearedDay 5–7: tune autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit on billing_prod — aggressive freeze.
Day 8: xid_age down to 1.4 billion. Green zone.
Cost: 3 nights DBA overtime, 12% elevated I/O, zero write shutdown.
Cheaper than billing_prod refusing writes at 2 AM.
→ Your Database Is Bleeding Money. The Incident Playbook. — VACUUM/bloat/wraparound section with decision tree for 3 AM. Same queries, context for when yellow means nine days.
Autovacuum settings we changed permanently
ALTER DATABASE billing_prod SET autovacuum_freeze_max_age = 1500000000;
ALTER TABLE billing_events SET (
autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.02,
autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit = 2000
);Plus: alert pages at 1.6B, not 1.9B. PagerDuty SEV3 minimum, escalates to SEV2 at 1.85B.
P3 at 1.98B is malpractice. We fixed it.
Long transactions — the real enemy
Wraparound warnings are symptoms. Blockers are disease.
→ Analytics replication slot held xmin 11 days — dropped and rebuilt → BI tool default: no statement timeout — set statement_timeout = '30min' → Staging cron pointed at prod (separate incident) — killed
SELECT slot_name, age(xmin) FROM pg_replication_slots;
-- analytics_slot | 847291034 -- fixedNo slot hygiene, no vacuum progress, no amount of Grafana yellow saves you.
Monitoring we ship now
Weekly cron (not human memory):
psql -t -c "SELECT MAX(age(datfrozenxid)) FROM pg_database;" | \
awk '{ if ($1 > 1600000000) print "WRAP RISK: " $1 }' | \
mail -s "pg-wrap-check" dba-oncall@company.comGrafana: trend line 90 days, not instant value. You want slope, not snapshot.
Six-week yellow was a trend screaming. We looked at color, not angle.
What shutdown would have looked like
At 2 billion XIDs Postgres stops accepting writes. Read-only mode at best. Full single-user at worst.
billing_prod write stop = $47,000/hour revenue at our run rate. 11 days sounded far. 9 days was the math at ingest acceleration.
We ran tabletop: “wraparound fires tonight.” Answer was ugly without Day 1–8 sprint already done.
Replication slot audit — monthly now
SELECT slot_name, active, age(xmin), pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn)) AS retained_wal
FROM pg_replication_slots
ORDER BY age(xmin) DESC;Any age(xmin) > 500000000 pages DBA. Analytics slot was 847 million — Tuesday blocker, Sunday prevention.
Dead tuples — the silent accelerator
billing_events was 40% dead tuples when we started the sprint. Autovacuum couldn't keep pace with insert rate plus long xmin hold.
SELECT relname, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup,
round(100.0 * n_dead_tup / NULLIF(n_live_tup + n_dead_tup, 0), 2) AS dead_pct
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE relname = 'billing_events';
-- dead_pct: 40.12Dead tuples don’t directly cause wraparound. They cause autovacuum to spend cycles on garbage instead of freeze. VACUUM FREEZE on bloated table took 3 nights instead of 1.
Lesson: wraparound alert is also bloat alert. Fix blockers, then freeze, then tune autovacuum — in that order.
The P3 ticket comment that aged poorly
Ticket comment from February 14: “Autovacuum will handle this, revisit next quarter.”
March 21 would have been next quarter. Shutdown window March 19–22 by our math.
Revisit cadence for wraparound warnings: weekly until xid_age < 1.2B, then monthly. Not quarterly. Not "when someone remembers."
-- now on weekly DBA report
SELECT datname, age(datfrozenxid),
round(100.0 * age(datfrozenxid) / 2000000000, 1) AS pct_to_limit
FROM pg_database ORDER BY 2 DESC;billing_prod at 99.1% to limit when I got paged. Percent framing scared finance more than raw billions. Sometimes the right unit is the one that gets budget.
Vacuum progress monitoring — watching freeze happen
SELECT phase, heap_blks_total, heap_blks_scanned,
round(100.0 * heap_blks_scanned / NULLIF(heap_blks_total, 0), 2) AS pct
FROM pg_stat_progress_vacuum
WHERE relid = 'billing_events'::regclass;Nightly 4-hour windows showed progress: 12%, 28%, 41%… Finance could see we weren’t idle. Budget approved for contracted DBA because graphs existed.
Without progress visibility, wraparound sprint looks like ops crying wolf. With it, wolf is measurable.
Black Friday rehearsal — why nine days wasn’t eleven
Ingest rate jumped 40% during load test rehearsal. xid_age velocity increased. 11 days became 9.
Alert fired red again. This time P1 ticket. DBA team already mid-sprint.
Without rehearsal: shutdown risk during actual Black Friday week. With rehearsal: sprint already Day 6 when velocity spike hit.
Load tests aren’t just for app latency. They’re for transaction ID consumption.
The autovacuum tuning spreadsheet
→ autovacuum_freeze_max_age: 200M → 150M (earlier freeze) → autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit: default → 2000 on billing_events → autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor: 0.2 → 0.02 on hot tables → maintenance_work_mem: 64MB → 1GB during freeze windows
Each change documented with rollback. DBA sign-off per parameter. Boring. Kept billing_prod writing.
When the DBA got paged — and why it mattered
xid_age at 1.98B triggered manual page despite P3 ticket. DBA on-call — me — saw ticket history: six weeks of “revisit next quarter.”
Escalation to VP at 1.85B now automatic. Nobody waits for human optimism at 1.98B.
9-day sprint approved in one meeting because shutdown cost ($47k/hour) was on slide one.
pg_cron weekly wrap check — installed
SELECT cron.schedule('wraparound-check', '0 6 * * 1',
$$SELECT CASE WHEN MAX(age(datfrozenxid)) > 1600000000
THEN pg_notify('dba_alerts', 'wraparound risk') END
FROM pg_database$$);Database notifies us. We don’t rely on humans reading yellow panels. Six-week yellow was panel failure. Cron is backstop.
xid_age at sprint end — the number that mattered
Day 8: xid_age 1.4 billion. 30% headroom restored.
Day 9: load test would have consumed 11 days remaining. We had weeks.
Wraparound is slow until it’s instant. Trend lines lie until slope bends vertical. We bent it back.
The P3 ticket closure comment
Closed with: “Resolved via emergency VACUUM sprint. Alert thresholds updated. Do not reopen without DBA review.”
Six weeks of deferral compressed into nine days of urgency. Ticket age is not risk assessment. xid_age is.

Postgres wraparound is a Froquiz database scenario until billing_prod hits 1.98 billion XIDs — drill the VACUUM FREEZE path before the P3 ticket becomes a shutdown. → Froquiz
